  4â†’
  5â†’package kotlinx.coroutines.test
  6â†’
  7â†’import kotlinx.atomicfu.*
  8â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.channels.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.channels.Channel.Factory.CONFLATED
 11â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.internal.*
 12â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.selects.*
 13â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
 14â†’import kotlin.jvm.*
 15â†’import kotlin.time.*
 16â†’import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.milliseconds
 17â†’
 18â†’/**
 19â†’ * This is a scheduler for coroutines used in tests, providing the delay-skipping behavior.
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * [Test dispatchers][TestDispatcher] are parameterized with a scheduler. Several dispatchers can share the
 22â†’ * same scheduler, in which case their knowledge about the virtual time will be synchronized. When the dispatchers
 23â†’ * require scheduling an event at a later point in time, they notify the scheduler, which will establish the order of
 24â†’ * the tasks.
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * The scheduler can be queried to advance the time (via [advanceTimeBy]), run all the scheduled tasks advancing the
 27â†’ * virtual time as needed (via [advanceUntilIdle]), or run the tasks that are scheduled to run as soon as possible but
 28â†’ * haven't yet been dispatched (via [runCurrent]).
 29â†’ */
 30â†’public class TestCoroutineScheduler : AbstractCoroutineContextElement(TestCoroutineScheduler),
 31â†’    CoroutineContext.Element {
 32â†’
 33â†’    /** @suppress */
 34â†’    public companion object Key : CoroutineContext.Key<TestCoroutineScheduler>
 35â†’
 36â†’    /** This heap stores the knowledge about which dispatchers are interested in which moments of virtual time. */
 37â†’    // TODO: all the synchronization is done via a separate lock, so a non-thread-safe priority queue can be used.
 38â†’    private val events = ThreadSafeHeap<TestDispatchEvent<Any>>()
 39â†’
 40â†’    /** Establishes that [currentTime] can't exceed the time of the earliest event in [events]. */
 41â†’    private val lock = SynchronizedObject()
 42â†’
 43â†’    /** This counter establishes some order on the events that happen at the same virtual time. */
 44â†’    private val count = atomic(0L)
 45â†’
 46â†’    /** The current virtual time in milliseconds. */
 47â†’    @ExperimentalCoroutinesApi
 48â†’    public var currentTime: Long = 0
 49â†’        get() = synchronized(lock) { field }
 50â†’        private set
 51â†’
 52â†’    /** A channel for notifying about the fact that a foreground work dispatch recently happened. */
 53â†’    private val dispatchEventsForeground: Channel<Unit> = Channel(CONFLATED)
 54â†’
 55â†’    /** A channel for notifying about the fact that a dispatch recently happened. */
 56â†’    private val dispatchEvents: Channel<Unit> = Channel(CONFLATED)
 57â†’
 58â†’    /**
 59â†’     * Registers a request for the scheduler to notify [dispatcher] at a virtual moment [timeDeltaMillis] milliseconds
 60â†’     * later via [TestDispatcher.processEvent], which will be called with the provided [marker] object.
 61â†’     *
 62â†’     * Returns the handler which can be used to cancel the registration.
 63â†’     */
 64â†’    internal fun <T : Any> registerEvent(
 65â†’        dispatcher: TestDispatcher,
 66â†’        timeDeltaMillis: Long,
 67â†’        marker: T,
 68â†’        context: CoroutineContext,
 69â†’        isCancelled: (T) -> Boolean
 70â†’    ): DisposableHandle {
 71â†’        require(timeDeltaMillis >= 0) { "Attempted scheduling an event earlier in time (with the time delta $timeDeltaMillis)" }
 72â†’        checkSchedulerInContext(this, context)
 73â†’        val count = count.getAndIncrement()
 74â†’        val isForeground = context[BackgroundWork] === null
 75â†’        return synchronized(lock) {
 76â†’            val time = addClamping(currentTime, timeDeltaMillis)
 77â†’            val event = TestDispatchEvent(dispatcher, count, time, marker as Any, isForeground) { isCancelled(marker) }
 78â†’            events.addLast(event)
 79â†’            /** can't be moved above: otherwise, [onDispatchEventForeground] or [onDispatchEvent] could consume the
 80â†’             * token sent here before there's actually anything in the event queue. */
 81â†’            sendDispatchEvent(context)
 82â†’            DisposableHandle {
 83â†’                synchronized(lock) {
 84â†’                    events.remove(event)
 85â†’                }
 86â†’            }
 87â†’        }
 88â†’    }
 89â†’
 90â†’    /**
 91â†’     * Runs the next enqueued task, advancing the virtual time to the time of its scheduled awakening,
 92â†’     * unless [condition] holds.
 93â†’     */
 94â†’    internal fun tryRunNextTaskUnless(condition: () -> Boolean): Boolean {
 95â†’        val event = synchronized(lock) {
 96â†’            if (condition()) return false
 97â†’            val event = events.removeFirstOrNull() ?: return false
 98â†’            if (currentTime > event.time)
 99â†’                currentTimeAheadOfEvents()
100â†’            currentTime = event.time
101â†’            event
102â†’        }
103â†’        event.dispatcher.processEvent(event.marker)
104â†’        return true
105â†’    }
106â†’
107â†’    /**
108â†’     * Runs the enqueued tasks in the specified order, advancing the virtual time as needed until there are no more
109â†’     * tasks associated with the dispatchers linked to this scheduler.
110â†’     *
111â†’     * A breaking change from `TestCoroutineDispatcher.advanceTimeBy` is that it no longer returns the total number of
112â†’     * milliseconds by which the execution of this method has advanced the virtual time. If you want to recreate that
113â†’     * functionality, query [currentTime] before and after the execution to achieve the same result.
114â†’     */
115â†’    public fun advanceUntilIdle(): Unit = advanceUntilIdleOr { events.none(TestDispatchEvent<*>::isForeground) }
116â†’
117â†’    /**
118â†’     * [condition]: guaranteed to be invoked under the lock.
119â†’     */
120â†’    internal fun advanceUntilIdleOr(condition: () -> Boolean) {
121â†’        while (true) {
122â†’            if (!tryRunNextTaskUnless(condition))
123â†’                return
124â†’        }
125â†’    }
126â†’
127â†’    /**
128â†’     * Runs the tasks that are scheduled to execute at this moment of virtual time.
129â†’     */
130â†’    public fun runCurrent() {
131â†’        val timeMark = synchronized(lock) { currentTime }
132â†’        while (true) {
133â†’            val event = synchronized(lock) {
134â†’                events.removeFirstIf { it.time <= timeMark } ?: return
135â†’            }
136â†’            event.dispatcher.processEvent(event.marker)
137â†’        }
138â†’    }
139â†’
140â†’    /**
141â†’     * Moves the virtual clock of this dispatcher forward by [the specified amount][delayTimeMillis], running the
142â†’     * scheduled tasks in the meantime.
143â†’     *
144â†’     * Breaking changes from [TestCoroutineDispatcher.advanceTimeBy]:
145â†’     * * Intentionally doesn't return a `Long` value, as its use cases are unclear. We may restore it in the future;
146â†’     *   please describe your use cases at [the issue tracker](https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines/issues/).
147â†’     *   For now, it's possible to query [currentTime] before and after execution of this method, to the same effect.
148â†’     * * It doesn't run the tasks that are scheduled at exactly [currentTime] + [delayTimeMillis]. For example,
149â†’     *   advancing the time by one millisecond used to run the tasks at the current millisecond *and* the next
150â†’     *   millisecond, but now will stop just before executing any task starting at the next millisecond.
151â†’     * * Overflowing the target time used to lead to nothing being done, but will now run the tasks scheduled at up to
152â†’     *   (but not including) [Long.MAX_VALUE].
153â†’     *
154â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if passed a negative [delay][delayTimeMillis].
155â†’     */
156â†’    @ExperimentalCoroutinesApi
157â†’    public fun advanceTimeBy(delayTimeMillis: Long): Unit = advanceTimeBy(delayTimeMillis.milliseconds)
158â†’
159â†’    /**
160â†’     * Moves the virtual clock of this dispatcher forward by [the specified amount][delayTime], running the
161â†’     * scheduled tasks in the meantime.
162â†’     *
163â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if passed a negative [delay][delayTime].
164â†’     */
165â†’    public fun advanceTimeBy(delayTime: Duration) {
166â†’        require(!delayTime.isNegative()) { "Can not advance time by a negative delay: $delayTime" }
167â†’        val startingTime = currentTime
168â†’        val targetTime = addClamping(startingTime, delayTime.inWholeMilliseconds)
169â†’        while (true) {
170â†’            val event = synchronized(lock) {
171â†’                val timeMark = currentTime
172â†’                val event = events.removeFirstIf { targetTime > it.time }
173â†’                when {
174â†’                    event == null -> {
175â†’                        currentTime = targetTime
176â†’                        return
177â†’                    }
178â†’                    timeMark > event.time -> currentTimeAheadOfEvents()
179â†’                    else -> {
180â†’                        currentTime = event.time
181â†’                        event
182â†’                    }
183â†’                }
184â†’            }
185â†’            event.dispatcher.processEvent(event.marker)
186â†’        }
187â†’    }
188â†’
189â†’    /**
190â†’     * Checks that the only tasks remaining in the scheduler are cancelled.
191â†’     */
192â†’    internal fun isIdle(strict: Boolean = true): Boolean =
193â†’        synchronized(lock) {
194â†’            if (strict) events.isEmpty else events.none { !it.isCancelled() }
195â†’        }
196â†’
197â†’    /**
198â†’     * Notifies this scheduler about a dispatch event.
199â†’     *
200â†’     * [context] is the context in which the task will be dispatched.
201â†’     */
202â†’    internal fun sendDispatchEvent(context: CoroutineContext) {
203â†’        dispatchEvents.trySend(Unit)
204â†’        if (context[BackgroundWork] !== BackgroundWork)
205â†’            dispatchEventsForeground.trySend(Unit)
206â†’    }
207â†’
208â†’    /**
209â†’     * Waits for a notification about a dispatch event.
210â†’     */
211â†’    internal suspend fun receiveDispatchEvent() = dispatchEvents.receive()
212â†’
213â†’    /**
214â†’     * Consumes the knowledge that a dispatch event happened recently.
215â†’     */
216â†’    internal val onDispatchEvent: SelectClause1<Unit> get() = dispatchEvents.onReceive
217â†’
218â†’    /**
219â†’     * Consumes the knowledge that a foreground work dispatch event happened recently.
220â†’     */
221â†’    internal val onDispatchEventForeground: SelectClause1<Unit> get() = dispatchEventsForeground.onReceive
222â†’
223â†’    /**
224â†’     * Returns the [TimeSource] representation of the virtual time of this scheduler.
225â†’     */
226â†’    @ExperimentalTime
227â†’    public val timeSource: TimeSource.WithComparableMarks = object : AbstractLongTimeSource(DurationUnit.MILLISECONDS) {
228â†’        override fun read(): Long = currentTime
229â†’    }
230â†’}
231â†’
232â†’// Some error-throwing functions for pretty stack traces
233â†’private fun currentTimeAheadOfEvents(): Nothing = invalidSchedulerState()
234â†’
235â†’private fun invalidSchedulerState(): Nothing =
236â†’    throw IllegalStateException("The test scheduler entered an invalid state. Please report this at https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines/issues.")
237â†’
238â†’/** [ThreadSafeHeap] node representing a scheduled task, ordered by the planned execution time. */
239â†’private class TestDispatchEvent<T>(
240â†’    @JvmField val dispatcher: TestDispatcher,
241â†’    private val count: Long,
242â†’    @JvmField val time: Long,
243â†’    @JvmField val marker: T,
244â†’    @JvmField val isForeground: Boolean,
245â†’    // TODO: remove once the deprecated API is gone
246â†’    @JvmField val isCancelled: () -> Boolean
247â†’) : Comparable<TestDispatchEvent<*>>, ThreadSafeHeapNode {
248â†’    override var heap: ThreadSafeHeap<*>? = null
249â†’    override var index: Int = 0
250â†’
251â†’    override fun compareTo(other: TestDispatchEvent<*>) =
252â†’        compareValuesBy(this, other, TestDispatchEvent<*>::time, TestDispatchEvent<*>::count)
253â†’
254â†’    override fun toString() = "TestDispatchEvent(time=$time, dispatcher=$dispatcher${if (isForeground) "" else ", background"})"
255â†’}
256â†’
257â†’// works with positive `a`, `b`
258â†’private fun addClamping(a: Long, b: Long): Long = (a + b).let { if (it >= 0) it else Long.MAX_VALUE }
259â†’
260â†’internal fun checkSchedulerInContext(scheduler: TestCoroutineScheduler, context: CoroutineContext) {
261â†’    context[TestCoroutineScheduler]?.let {
262â†’        check(it === scheduler) {
263â†’            "Detected use of different schedulers. If you need to use several test coroutine dispatchers, " +
264â†’                "create one `TestCoroutineScheduler` and pass it to each of them."
265â†’        }
266â†’    }
267â†’}
268â†’
269â†’/**
270â†’ * A coroutine context key denoting that the work is to be executed in the background.
271â†’ * @see [TestScope.backgroundScope]
272â†’ */
273â†’internal object BackgroundWork : CoroutineContext.Key<BackgroundWork>, CoroutineContext.Element {
274â†’    override val key: CoroutineContext.Key<*>
275â†’        get() = this
276â†’
277â†’    override fun toString(): String = "BackgroundWork"
278â†’}
279â†’
280â†’private fun<T> ThreadSafeHeap<T>.none(predicate: (T) -> Boolean) where T: ThreadSafeHeapNode, T: Comparable<T> =
281â†’    find(predicate) == null

180â†’     * chance to run in its dispatcher, so that the suspended function threw an exception
181â†’     * instead of returning this value.
182â†’     *
183â†’     * The installed [onCancellation] handler should not throw any exceptions.
184â†’     * If it does, they will get caught, wrapped into a [CompletionHandlerException] and
185â†’     * processed as an uncaught exception in the context of the current coroutine
186â†’     * (see [CoroutineExceptionHandler]).
187â†’     *
188â†’     * This function shall be used when resuming with a resource that must be closed by
189â†’     * code that called the corresponding suspending function, for example:
190â†’     *
191â†’     * ```
192â†’     * continuation.resume(resource) {
193â†’     *     resource.close()
194â†’     * }
195â†’     * ```
196â†’     *
197â†’     * A more complete example and further details are given in
198â†’     * the documentation for the [suspendCancellableCoroutine] function.
199â†’     *
200â†’     * **Note**: The [onCancellation] handler must be fast, non-blocking, and thread-safe.
201â†’     * It can be invoked concurrently with the surrounding code.
202â†’     * There is no guarantee on the execution context of its invocation.
203â†’     */
204â†’    @ExperimentalCoroutinesApi // since 1.2.0
205â†’    public fun resume(value: T, onCancellation: ((cause: Throwable) -> Unit)?)
206â†’}
207â†’
208â†’/**
209â†’ * Suspends the coroutine like [suspendCoroutine], but providing a [CancellableContinuation] to
210â†’ * the [block]. This function throws a [CancellationException] if the [Job] of the coroutine is
211â†’ * cancelled or completed while it is suspended.
212â†’ *
213â†’ * A typical use of this function is to suspend a coroutine while waiting for a result
214â†’ * from a single-shot callback API and to return the result to the caller.
215â†’ * For multi-shot callback APIs see [callbackFlow][kotlinx.coroutines.flow.callbackFlow].
216â†’ *
217â†’ * ```
218â†’ * suspend fun awaitCallback(): T = suspendCancellableCoroutine { continuation ->
219â†’ *     val callback = object : Callback { // Implementation of some callback interface
220â†’ *         override fun onCompleted(value: T) {
221â†’ *             // Resume coroutine with a value provided by the callback
222â†’ *             continuation.resume(value)
223â†’ *         }
224â†’ *         override fun onApiError(cause: Throwable) {
225â†’ *             // Resume coroutine with an exception provided by the callback
226â†’ *             continuation.resumeWithException(cause)
227â†’ *         }
228â†’ *     }
229â†’ *     // Register callback with an API
230â†’ *     api.register(callback)
231â†’ *     // Remove callback on cancellation
232â†’ *     continuation.invokeOnCancellation { api.unregister(callback) }
233â†’ *     // At this point the coroutine is suspended by suspendCancellableCoroutine until callback fires
234â†’ * }
235â†’ * ```
236â†’ *
237â†’ * > The callback `register`/`unregister` methods provided by an external API must be thread-safe, because
238â†’ * > `invokeOnCancellation` block can be called at any time due to asynchronous nature of cancellation, even
239â†’ * > concurrently with the call of the callback.
240â†’ *
241â†’ * ### Prompt cancellation guarantee
242â†’ *
243â†’ * This function provides **prompt cancellation guarantee**.
244â†’ * If the [Job] of the current coroutine was cancelled while this function was suspended it will not resume
245â†’ * successfully.
246â†’ *
247â†’ * The cancellation of the coroutine's job is generally asynchronous with respect to the suspended coroutine.
248â†’ * The suspended coroutine is resumed with the call it to its [Continuation.resumeWith] member function or to
249â†’ * [resume][Continuation.resume] extension function.
250â†’ * However, when coroutine is resumed, it does not immediately start executing, but is passed to its
251â†’ * [CoroutineDispatcher] to schedule its execution when dispatcher's resources become available for execution.
252â†’ * The job's cancellation can happen both before, after, and concurrently with the call to `resume`. In any
253â†’ * case, prompt cancellation guarantees that the the coroutine will not resume its code successfully.
254â†’ *
255â†’ * If the coroutine was resumed with an exception (for example, using [Continuation.resumeWithException] extension
256â†’ * function) and cancelled, then the resulting exception of the `suspendCancellableCoroutine` function is determined
257â†’ * by whichever action (exceptional resume or cancellation) that happened first.
258â†’ *
259â†’ * ### Returning resources from a suspended coroutine
260â†’ *
261â†’ * As a result of a prompt cancellation guarantee, when a closeable resource
262â†’ * (like open file or a handle to another native resource) is returned from a suspended coroutine as a value
263â†’ * it can be lost when the coroutine is cancelled. In order to ensure that the resource can be properly closed
264â†’ * in this case, the [CancellableContinuation] interface provides two functions.
265â†’ *
266â†’ * * [invokeOnCancellation][CancellableContinuation.invokeOnCancellation] installs a handler that is called
267â†’ *   whenever a suspend coroutine is being cancelled. In addition to the example at the beginning, it can be
268â†’ *   used to ensure that a resource that was opened before the call to
269â†’ *   `suspendCancellableCoroutine` or in its body is closed in case of cancellation.
270â†’ *
271â†’ * ```
272â†’ * suspendCancellableCoroutine { continuation ->
273â†’ *    val resource = openResource() // Opens some resource
274â†’ *    continuation.invokeOnCancellation {
275â†’ *        resource.close() // Ensures the resource is closed on cancellation
276â†’ *    }
277â†’ *    // ...
278â†’ * }
279â†’ * ```

300â†’ * ### Implementation details and custom continuation interceptors
301â†’ *
302â†’ * The prompt cancellation guarantee is the result of a coordinated implementation inside `suspendCancellableCoroutine`
303â†’ * function and the [CoroutineDispatcher] class. The coroutine dispatcher checks for the status of the [Job] immediately
304â†’ * before continuing its normal execution and aborts this normal execution, calling all the corresponding
305â†’ * cancellation handlers, if the job was cancelled.
306â†’ *
307â†’ * If a custom implementation of [ContinuationInterceptor] is used in a coroutine's context that does not extend
308â†’ * [CoroutineDispatcher] class, then there is no prompt cancellation guarantee. A custom continuation interceptor
309â†’ * can resume execution of a previously suspended coroutine even if its job was already cancelled.
310â†’ */
311â†’public suspend inline fun <T> suspendCancellableCoroutine(
312â†’    crossinline block: (CancellableContinuation<T>) -> Unit
313â†’): T =
314â†’    suspendCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn { uCont ->
315â†’        val cancellable = CancellableContinuationImpl(uCont.intercepted(), resumeMode = MODE_CANCELLABLE)
316â†’        /*
317â†’         * For non-atomic cancellation we setup parent-child relationship immediately
318â†’         * in case when `block` blocks the current thread (e.g. Rx2 with trampoline scheduler), but
319â†’         * properly supports cancellation.
320â†’         */
321â†’        cancellable.initCancellability()
322â†’        block(cancellable)
323â†’        cancellable.getResult()
324â†’    }
325â†’
326â†’/**
327â†’ * Suspends the coroutine similar to [suspendCancellableCoroutine], but an instance of
328â†’ * [CancellableContinuationImpl] is reused.
329â†’ */
330â†’internal suspend inline fun <T> suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable(
331â†’    crossinline block: (CancellableContinuationImpl<T>) -> Unit
332â†’): T = suspendCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn { uCont ->
333â†’    val cancellable = getOrCreateCancellableContinuation(uCont.intercepted())
334â†’    try {
335â†’        block(cancellable)
336â†’    } catch (e: Throwable) {
337â†’        // Here we catch any unexpected exception from user-supplied block (e.g. invariant violation)
338â†’        // and release claimed continuation in order to leave it in a reasonable state (see #3613)
339â†’        cancellable.releaseClaimedReusableContinuation()
340â†’        throw e
341â†’    }
342â†’    cancellable.getResult()
343â†’}
344â†’
345â†’internal fun <T> getOrCreateCancellableContinuation(delegate: Continuation<T>): CancellableContinuationImpl<T> {
346â†’    // If used outside our dispatcher
347â†’    if (delegate !is DispatchedContinuation<T>) {
348â†’        return CancellableContinuationImpl(delegate, MODE_CANCELLABLE)
349â†’    }
350â†’    /*
351â†’     * Attempt to claim reusable instance.
352â†’     *
353â†’     * suspendCancellableCoroutineReusable { // <- claimed
354â†’     *     // Any asynchronous cancellation is "postponed" while this block
355â†’     *     // is being executed
356â†’     * } // postponed cancellation is checked here.
357â†’     *
358â†’     * Claim can fail for the following reasons:
359â†’     * 1) Someone tried to make idempotent resume.
360â†’     *    Idempotent resume is internal (used only by us) and is used only in `select`,
361â†’     *    thus leaking CC instance for indefinite time.
362â†’     * 2) Continuation was cancelled. Then we should prevent any further reuse and bail out.
363â†’     */
364â†’    return delegate.claimReusableCancellableContinuation()?.takeIf { it.resetStateReusable() }
365â†’        ?: return CancellableContinuationImpl(delegate, MODE_CANCELLABLE_REUSABLE)
366â†’}
367â†’
368â†’/**
369â†’ * Disposes the specified [handle] when this continuation is cancelled.
370â†’ *
371â†’ * This is a shortcut for the following code with slightly more efficient implementation (one fewer object created):
372â†’ * ```
373â†’ * invokeOnCancellation { handle.dispose() }
374â†’ * ```
375â†’ *
376â†’ * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
377â†’ */
378â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
379â†’public fun CancellableContinuation<*>.disposeOnCancellation(handle: DisposableHandle): Unit =
380â†’    invokeOnCancellation(handler = DisposeOnCancel(handle).asHandler)
381â†’
382â†’private class DisposeOnCancel(private val handle: DisposableHandle) : CancelHandler() {
383â†’    override fun invoke(cause: Throwable?) = handle.dispose()
384â†’    override fun toString(): String = "DisposeOnCancel[$handle]"
385â†’}
386â†’

1â†’/*

  4â†’
  5â†’package kotlinx.coroutines.flow
  6â†’
  7â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
  8â†’import org.junit.*
  9â†’import org.junit.Test
 10â†’import java.util.*
 11â†’import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*
 12â†’import kotlin.random.*
 13â†’import kotlin.test.*
 14â†’import kotlin.time.*
 15â†’import kotlin.time.TimeSource
 16â†’
 17â†’@OptIn(ExperimentalTime::class)
 18â†’class SharingStressTest : TestBase() {
 19â†’    private val testDuration = 1000L * stressTestMultiplier
 20â†’    private val nSubscribers = 5
 21â†’    private val testStarted = TimeSource.Monotonic.markNow()
 22â†’
 23â†’    @get:Rule
 24â†’    val emitterDispatcher = ExecutorRule(1)
 25â†’    
 26â†’    @get:Rule
 27â†’    val subscriberDispatcher = ExecutorRule(nSubscribers)
 28â†’
 29â†’    @Test
 30â†’    public fun testNoReplayLazy() =
 31â†’        testStress(0, started = SharingStarted.Lazily)
 32â†’
 33â†’    @Test
 34â†’    public fun testNoReplayWhileSubscribed() =
 35â†’        testStress(0, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed())
 36â†’
 37â†’    @Test
 38â†’    public fun testNoReplayWhileSubscribedTimeout() =
 39â†’        testStress(0, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(stopTimeoutMillis = 50L))
 40â†’
 41â†’    @Test
 42â†’    public fun testReplay100WhileSubscribed() =
 43â†’        testStress(100, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed())
 44â†’
 45â†’    @Test
 46â†’    public fun testReplay100WhileSubscribedReset() =                             
 47â†’        testStress(100, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(replayExpirationMillis = 0L))
 48â†’
 49â†’    @Test
 50â†’    public fun testReplay100WhileSubscribedTimeout() =
 51â†’        testStress(100, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(stopTimeoutMillis = 50L))
 52â†’
 53â†’    @Test
 54â†’    public fun testStateLazy() =
 55â†’        testStress(1, started = SharingStarted.Lazily)
 56â†’
 57â†’    @Test
 58â†’    public fun testStateWhileSubscribed() =
 59â†’        testStress(1, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed())
 60â†’
 61â†’    @Test
 62â†’    public fun testStateWhileSubscribedReset() =
 63â†’        testStress(1, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(replayExpirationMillis = 0L))
 64â†’
 65â†’    private fun testStress(replay: Int, started: SharingStarted) = runTest {
 66â†’        log("-- Stress with replay=$replay, started=$started")
 67â†’        val random = Random(1)
 68â†’        val emitIndex = AtomicLong()
 69â†’        val cancelledEmits = HashSet<Long>()
 70â†’        val missingCollects = Collections.synchronizedSet(LinkedHashSet<Long>())
 71â†’        // at most one copy of upstream can be running at any time
 72â†’        val isRunning = AtomicInteger(0)
 73â†’        val upstream = flow {
 74â†’            assertEquals(0, isRunning.getAndIncrement())
 75â†’            try {
 76â†’                while (true) {
 77â†’                    val value = emitIndex.getAndIncrement()
 78â†’                    try {
 79â†’                        emit(value)
 80â†’                    } catch (e: CancellationException) {
 81â†’                        // emission was cancelled -> could be missing
 82â†’                        cancelledEmits.add(value)
 83â†’                        throw e
 84â†’                    }
 85â†’                }
 86â†’            } finally {
 87â†’                assertEquals(1, isRunning.getAndDecrement())
 88â†’            }
 89â†’        }
 90â†’        val subCount = MutableStateFlow(0)
 91â†’        val sharingJob = Job()
 92â†’        val sharingScope = this + emitterDispatcher + sharingJob
 93â†’        val usingStateFlow = replay == 1
 94â†’        val sharedFlow = if (usingStateFlow)
 95â†’            upstream.stateIn(sharingScope, started, 0L)
 96â†’        else
 97â†’            upstream.shareIn(sharingScope, started, replay)
 98â†’        try {
 99â†’            val subscribers = ArrayList<SubJob>()
100â†’            withTimeoutOrNull(testDuration) {
101â†’                // start and stop subscribers
102â†’                while (true) {
103â†’                    log("Staring $nSubscribers subscribers")
104â†’                    repeat(nSubscribers) {
105â†’                        subscribers += launchSubscriber(sharedFlow, usingStateFlow, subCount, missingCollects)
106â†’                    }
107â†’                    // wait until they all subscribed
108â†’                    subCount.first { it == nSubscribers }
109â†’                    // let them work a bit more & make sure emitter did not hang
110â†’                    val fromEmitIndex = emitIndex.get()
111â†’                    val waitEmitIndex = fromEmitIndex + 100 // wait until 100 emitted
112â†’                    withTimeout(10000) { // wait for at most 10s for something to be emitted
113â†’                        do {
114â†’                            delay(random.nextLong(50L..100L))
115â†’                        } while (emitIndex.get() < waitEmitIndex)  // Ok, enough was emitted, wait more if not
116â†’                    }
117â†’                    // Stop all subscribers and ensure they collected something
118â†’                    log("Stopping subscribers (emitted = ${emitIndex.get() - fromEmitIndex})")
119â†’                    subscribers.forEach {
120â†’                        it.job.cancelAndJoin()
121â†’                        assertTrue { it.count > 0 } // something must be collected too
122â†’                    }
123â†’                    subscribers.clear()
124â†’                    log("Intermission")
125â†’                    delay(random.nextLong(10L..100L)) // wait a bit before starting them again
126â†’                }
127â†’            }
128â†’            if (!subscribers.isEmpty()) {
129â†’                log("Stopping subscribers")
130â†’                subscribers.forEach { it.job.cancelAndJoin() }
131â†’            }
132â†’        } finally {
133â†’            log("--- Finally: Cancelling sharing job")
134â†’            sharingJob.cancel()
135â†’        }
136â†’        sharingJob.join() // make sure sharing job did not hang
137â†’        log("Emitter was cancelled ${cancelledEmits.size} times")
138â†’        log("Collectors missed ${missingCollects.size} values")
139â†’        for (value in missingCollects) {
140â†’            assertTrue(value in cancelledEmits, "Value $value is missing for no apparent reason")
141â†’        }
142â†’    }
143â†’
144â†’    private fun CoroutineScope.launchSubscriber(
145â†’        sharedFlow: SharedFlow<Long>,
146â†’        usingStateFlow: Boolean,
147â†’        subCount: MutableStateFlow<Int>,
148â†’        missingCollects: MutableSet<Long>
149â†’    ): SubJob {
150â†’        val subJob = SubJob()
151â†’        subJob.job = launch(subscriberDispatcher) {
152â†’            var last = -1L
153â†’            sharedFlow
154â†’                .onSubscription {
155â†’                    subCount.increment(1)
156â†’                }
157â†’                .onCompletion {
158â†’                    subCount.increment(-1)
159â†’                }
160â†’                .collect { j ->
161â†’                    subJob.count++
162â†’                    // last must grow sequentially, no jumping or losses
163â†’                    if (last == -1L) {
164â†’                        last = j
165â†’                    } else {
166â†’                        val expected = last + 1
167â†’                        if (usingStateFlow)
168â†’                            assertTrue(expected <= j)
169â†’                        else {
170â†’                            if (expected != j) {
171â†’                                if (j == expected + 1) {
172â†’                                    // if missing just one -- could be race with cancelled emit
173â†’                                    missingCollects.add(expected)
174â†’                                } else {
175â†’                                    // broken otherwise
176â†’                                    assertEquals(expected, j)
177â†’                                }
178â†’                            }
179â†’                        }
180â†’                        last = j
181â†’                    }
182â†’                }
183â†’        }
184â†’        return subJob
185â†’    }
186â†’
187â†’    private class SubJob {
188â†’        lateinit var job: Job
189â†’        var count = 0L
190â†’    }
191â†’
192â†’    private fun log(msg: String) = println("${testStarted.elapsedNow().inWholeMilliseconds} ms: $msg")
193â†’
194â†’    private fun MutableStateFlow<Int>.increment(delta: Int) {
195â†’        update { it + delta }
196â†’    }
197â†’}
198â†’

15â†’/**
16â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [kotlinx.coroutines.delay].
17â†’ */

19â†’
20â†’/**
21â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [kotlinx.coroutines.flow.debounce].
22â†’ */

24â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.debounce(timeout: Duration): Flow<T> = debounce(timeout.coerceToMillis())

26â†’/**
27â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [kotlinx.coroutines.flow.sample].
28â†’ */
29â†’@FlowPreview
30â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.sample(period: Duration): Flow<T> = sample(period.coerceToMillis())
31â†’
32â†’/**
33â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [SelectBuilder.onTimeout].
34â†’ */
35â†’public fun <R> SelectBuilder<R>.onTimeout(duration: Duration, block: suspend () -> R): Unit =
36â†’        onTimeout(duration.coerceToMillis(), block)
37â†’
38â†’/**
39â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [kotlinx.coroutines.withTimeout].
40â†’ */
41â†’public suspend fun <T> withTimeout(duration: Duration, block: suspend CoroutineScope.() -> T): T {
42â†’    contract {
43â†’        callsInPlace(block, InvocationKind.EXACTLY_ONCE)
44â†’    }
45â†’    return kotlinx.coroutines.withTimeout(duration.coerceToMillis(), block)
46â†’}
47â†’
48â†’/**
49â†’ * "java.time" adapter method for [kotlinx.coroutines.withTimeoutOrNull].
50â†’ */
51â†’public suspend fun <T> withTimeoutOrNull(duration: Duration, block: suspend CoroutineScope.() -> T): T? =
52â†’        kotlinx.coroutines.withTimeoutOrNull(duration.coerceToMillis(), block)
53â†’
54â†’/**
55â†’ * Coerces the given [Duration] to a millisecond delay.
56â†’ * Negative values are coerced to zero, values that cannot
57â†’ * be represented in milliseconds as long ("infinite" duration) are coerced to [Long.MAX_VALUE]
58â†’ * and durations lesser than a millisecond are coerced to 1 millisecond.
59â†’ *
60â†’ * The rationale of coercion:
61â†’ * 1) Too large durations typically indicate infinity and Long.MAX_VALUE is the
62â†’ *    best approximation of infinity we can provide.
63â†’ * 2) Coercing too small durations to 1 instead of 0 is crucial for two patterns:
64â†’ *    - Programming with deadlines and delays
65â†’ *    - Non-suspending fast-paths (e.g. `withTimeout(1 nanosecond) { 42 }` should not throw)
66â†’ */
67â†’private fun Duration.coerceToMillis(): Long {
68â†’    if (this <= Duration.ZERO) return 0
69â†’    if (this <= ChronoUnit.MILLIS.duration) return 1
70â†’
71â†’    // Maximum scalar values of Duration.ofMillis(Long.MAX_VALUE)
72â†’    val maxSeconds = 9223372036854775
73â†’    val maxNanos = 807000000
74â†’    return if (seconds < maxSeconds || seconds == maxSeconds && nano < maxNanos) toMillis()
75â†’    else Long.MAX_VALUE
76â†’}

1â†’/*

  4â†’
  5â†’package kotlinx.coroutines.channels
  6â†’
  7â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
  8â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
  9â†’
 10â†’/**
 11â†’ * Mode for [ticker] function.
 12â†’ *
 13â†’ * **Note: Ticker channels are not currently integrated with structured concurrency and their api will change in the future.**
 14â†’ */
 15â†’@ObsoleteCoroutinesApi
 16â†’public enum class TickerMode {
 17â†’    /**
 18â†’     * Adjust delay to maintain fixed period if consumer cannot keep up or is otherwise slow.
 19â†’     * **This is a default mode.**
 20â†’     *
 21â†’     * ```
 22â†’     * val channel = ticker(delay = 100)
 23â†’     * delay(350) // 250 ms late
 24â†’     * println(channel.tryReceive().getOrNull()) // prints Unit
 25â†’     * println(channel.tryReceive().getOrNull()) // prints null
 26â†’     *
 27â†’     * delay(50)
 28â†’     * println(channel.tryReceive().getOrNull()) // prints Unit, delay was adjusted
 29â†’     * delay(50)
 30â†’     * println(channel.tryReceive().getOrNull()) // prints null, we're not late relatively to previous element
 31â†’     * ```
 32â†’     */
 33â†’    FIXED_PERIOD,
 34â†’
 35â†’    /**
 36â†’     * Maintains fixed delay between produced elements if consumer cannot keep up or it otherwise slow.
 37â†’     */
 38â†’    FIXED_DELAY
 39â†’}
 40â†’
 41â†’/**
 42â†’ * Creates a channel that produces the first item after the given initial delay and subsequent items with the
 43â†’ * given delay between them.
 44â†’ *
 45â†’ * The resulting channel is a _rendezvous channel_. When receiver from this channel does not keep
 46â†’ * up with receiving the elements from this channel, they are not being sent due to backpressure. The actual
 47â†’ * timing behavior of ticker in this case is controlled by [mode] parameter which
 48â†’ * is set to [TickerMode.FIXED_PERIOD] by default. See [TickerMode] for other details.
 49â†’ *
 50â†’ * This channel stops producing elements immediately after [ReceiveChannel.cancel] invocation.
 51â†’ *
 52â†’ * **Note** producer to this channel is dispatched via [Dispatchers.Unconfined] by default and started eagerly.
 53â†’ *
 54â†’ * **Note: Ticker channels are not currently integrated with structured concurrency and their api will change in the future.**
 55â†’ *           
 56â†’ * @param delayMillis delay between each element in milliseconds.
 57â†’ * @param initialDelayMillis delay after which the first element will be produced (it is equal to [delayMillis] by default) in milliseconds.
 58â†’ * @param context context of the producing coroutine.
 59â†’ * @param mode specifies behavior when elements are not received ([FIXED_PERIOD][TickerMode.FIXED_PERIOD] by default).
 60â†’ */
 61â†’@ObsoleteCoroutinesApi
 62â†’public fun ticker(
 63â†’    delayMillis: Long,
 64â†’    initialDelayMillis: Long = delayMillis,
 65â†’    context: CoroutineContext = EmptyCoroutineContext,
 66â†’    mode: TickerMode = TickerMode.FIXED_PERIOD
 67â†’): ReceiveChannel<Unit> {
 68â†’    require(delayMillis >= 0) { "Expected non-negative delay, but has $delayMillis ms" }
 69â†’    require(initialDelayMillis >= 0) { "Expected non-negative initial delay, but has $initialDelayMillis ms" }
 70â†’    return GlobalScope.produce(Dispatchers.Unconfined + context, capacity = 0) {
 71â†’        when (mode) {
 72â†’            TickerMode.FIXED_PERIOD -> fixedPeriodTicker(delayMillis, initialDelayMillis, channel)
 73â†’            TickerMode.FIXED_DELAY -> fixedDelayTicker(delayMillis, initialDelayMillis, channel)
 74â†’        }
 75â†’    }
 76â†’}
 77â†’
 78â†’private suspend fun fixedPeriodTicker(
 79â†’    delayMillis: Long,
 80â†’    initialDelayMillis: Long,
 81â†’    channel: SendChannel<Unit>
 82â†’) {
 83â†’    var deadline = nanoTime() + delayToNanos(initialDelayMillis)
 84â†’    delay(initialDelayMillis)
 85â†’    val delayNs = delayToNanos(delayMillis)
 86â†’    while (true) {
 87â†’        deadline += delayNs
 88â†’        channel.send(Unit)
 89â†’        val now = nanoTime()
 90â†’        val nextDelay = (deadline - now).coerceAtLeast(0)
 91â†’        if (nextDelay == 0L && delayNs != 0L) {
 92â†’            val adjustedDelay = delayNs - (now - deadline) % delayNs
 93â†’            deadline = now + adjustedDelay
 94â†’            delay(delayNanosToMillis(adjustedDelay))
 95â†’        } else {
 96â†’            delay(delayNanosToMillis(nextDelay))
 97â†’        }
 98â†’    }
 99â†’}
100â†’
101â†’private suspend fun fixedDelayTicker(
102â†’    delayMillis: Long,
103â†’    initialDelayMillis: Long,
104â†’    channel: SendChannel<Unit>
105â†’) {
106â†’    delay(initialDelayMillis)
107â†’    while (true) {
108â†’        channel.send(Unit)
109â†’        delay(delayMillis)
110â†’    }
111â†’}
112â†’

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2016-2021 JetBrains s.r.o. Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 license.
  3â†’ */
  4â†’
  5â†’package kotlinx.coroutines
  6â†’
  7â†’import kotlinx.atomicfu.*
  8â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.internal.*
  9â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
 10â†’import kotlin.jvm.*
 11â†’
 12â†’/**
 13â†’ * Extended by [CoroutineDispatcher] implementations that have event loop inside and can
 14â†’ * be asked to process next event from their event queue.
 15â†’ *
 16â†’ * It may optionally implement [Delay] interface and support time-scheduled tasks.
 17â†’ * It is created or pigged back onto (see [ThreadLocalEventLoop])
 18â†’ * by `runBlocking` and by [Dispatchers.Unconfined].
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
 21â†’ */
 22â†’internal abstract class EventLoop : CoroutineDispatcher() {
 23â†’    /**
 24â†’     * Counts the number of nested `runBlocking` and [Dispatchers.Unconfined] that use this event loop.
 25â†’     */
 26â†’    private var useCount = 0L
 27â†’
 28â†’    /**
 29â†’     * Set to true on any use by `runBlocking`, because it potentially leaks this loop to other threads, so
 30â†’     * this instance must be properly shutdown. We don't need to shutdown event loop that was used solely
 31â†’     * by [Dispatchers.Unconfined] -- it can be left as [ThreadLocalEventLoop] and reused next time.
 32â†’     */
 33â†’    private var shared = false
 34â†’
 35â†’    /**
 36â†’     * Queue used by [Dispatchers.Unconfined] tasks.
 37â†’     * These tasks are thread-local for performance and take precedence over the rest of the queue.
 38â†’     */
 39â†’    private var unconfinedQueue: ArrayDeque<DispatchedTask<*>>? = null
 40â†’
 41â†’    /**
 42â†’     * Processes next event in this event loop.
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * The result of this function is to be interpreted like this:
 45â†’     * * `<= 0` -- there are potentially more events for immediate processing;
 46â†’     * * `> 0` -- a number of nanoseconds to wait for next scheduled event;
 47â†’     * * [Long.MAX_VALUE] -- no more events.
 48â†’     *
 49â†’     * **NOTE**: Must be invoked only from the event loop's thread
 50â†’     *          (no check for performance reasons, may be added in the future).
 51â†’     */
 52â†’    open fun processNextEvent(): Long {
 53â†’        if (!processUnconfinedEvent()) return Long.MAX_VALUE
 54â†’        return 0
 55â†’    }
 56â†’
 57â†’    protected open val isEmpty: Boolean get() = isUnconfinedQueueEmpty
 58â†’
 59â†’    protected open val nextTime: Long
 60â†’        get() {
 61â†’            val queue = unconfinedQueue ?: return Long.MAX_VALUE
 62â†’            return if (queue.isEmpty()) Long.MAX_VALUE else 0L
 63â†’        }
 64â†’
 65â†’    fun processUnconfinedEvent(): Boolean {
 66â†’        val queue = unconfinedQueue ?: return false
 67â†’        val task = queue.removeFirstOrNull() ?: return false
 68â†’        task.run()
 69â†’        return true
 70â†’    }
 71â†’    /**
 72â†’     * Returns `true` if the invoking `runBlocking(context) { ... }` that was passed this event loop in its context
 73â†’     * parameter should call [processNextEvent] for this event loop (otherwise, it will process thread-local one).
 74â†’     * By default, event loop implementation is thread-local and should not processed in the context
 75â†’     * (current thread's event loop should be processed instead).
 76â†’     */
 77â†’    open fun shouldBeProcessedFromContext(): Boolean = false
 78â†’
 79â†’    /**
 80â†’     * Dispatches task whose dispatcher returned `false` from [CoroutineDispatcher.isDispatchNeeded]
 81â†’     * into the current event loop.
 82â†’     */
 83â†’    fun dispatchUnconfined(task: DispatchedTask<*>) {
 84â†’        val queue = unconfinedQueue ?:
 85â†’            ArrayDeque<DispatchedTask<*>>().also { unconfinedQueue = it }
 86â†’        queue.addLast(task)
 87â†’    }
 88â†’
 89â†’    val isActive: Boolean
 90â†’        get() = useCount > 0
 91â†’
 92â†’    val isUnconfinedLoopActive: Boolean
 93â†’        get() = useCount >= delta(unconfined = true)
 94â†’
 95â†’    // May only be used from the event loop's thread
 96â†’    val isUnconfinedQueueEmpty: Boolean
 97â†’        get() = unconfinedQueue?.isEmpty() ?: true
 98â†’
 99â†’    private fun delta(unconfined: Boolean) =
100â†’        if (unconfined) (1L shl 32) else 1L
101â†’
102â†’    fun incrementUseCount(unconfined: Boolean = false) {
103â†’        useCount += delta(unconfined)
104â†’        if (!unconfined) shared = true 
105â†’    }
106â†’
107â†’    fun decrementUseCount(unconfined: Boolean = false) {
108â†’        useCount -= delta(unconfined)
109â†’        if (useCount > 0) return
110â†’        assert { useCount == 0L } // "Extra decrementUseCount"
111â†’        if (shared) {
112â†’            // shut it down and remove from ThreadLocalEventLoop
113â†’            shutdown()
114â†’        }
115â†’    }
116â†’
117â†’    final override fun limitedParallelism(parallelism: Int): CoroutineDispatcher {
118â†’        parallelism.checkParallelism()
119â†’        return this
120â†’    }
121â†’
122â†’    open fun shutdown() {}
123â†’}
124â†’
125â†’internal object ThreadLocalEventLoop {
126â†’    private val ref = commonThreadLocal<EventLoop?>(Symbol("ThreadLocalEventLoop"))
127â†’
128â†’    internal val eventLoop: EventLoop
129â†’        get() = ref.get() ?: createEventLoop().also { ref.set(it) }
130â†’
131â†’    internal fun currentOrNull(): EventLoop? =
132â†’        ref.get()
133â†’
134â†’    internal fun resetEventLoop() {
135â†’        ref.set(null)
136â†’    }
137â†’
138â†’    internal fun setEventLoop(eventLoop: EventLoop) {
139â†’        ref.set(eventLoop)
140â†’    }
141â†’}
142â†’
143â†’private val DISPOSED_TASK = Symbol("REMOVED_TASK")
144â†’
145â†’// results for scheduleImpl
146â†’private const val SCHEDULE_OK = 0
147â†’private const val SCHEDULE_COMPLETED = 1
148â†’private const val SCHEDULE_DISPOSED = 2
149â†’
150â†’private const val MS_TO_NS = 1_000_000L
151â†’private const val MAX_MS = Long.MAX_VALUE / MS_TO_NS
152â†’
153â†’/**
154â†’ * First-line overflow protection -- limit maximal delay.
155â†’ * Delays longer than this one (~146 years) are considered to be delayed "forever".
156â†’ */
157â†’private const val MAX_DELAY_NS = Long.MAX_VALUE / 2
158â†’
159â†’internal fun delayToNanos(timeMillis: Long): Long = when {
160â†’    timeMillis <= 0 -> 0L
161â†’    timeMillis >= MAX_MS -> Long.MAX_VALUE
162â†’    else -> timeMillis * MS_TO_NS
163â†’}
164â†’
165â†’internal fun delayNanosToMillis(timeNanos: Long): Long =
166â†’    timeNanos / MS_TO_NS
167â†’
168â†’private val CLOSED_EMPTY = Symbol("CLOSED_EMPTY")
169â†’
170â†’private typealias Queue<T> = LockFreeTaskQueueCore<T>
171â†’
172â†’internal expect abstract class EventLoopImplPlatform() : EventLoop {
173â†’    // Called to unpark this event loop's thread
174â†’    protected fun unpark()
175â†’
176â†’    // Called to reschedule to DefaultExecutor when this event loop is complete
177â†’    protected fun reschedule(now: Long, delayedTask: EventLoopImplBase.DelayedTask)
178â†’}
179â†’
180â†’internal abstract class EventLoopImplBase: EventLoopImplPlatform(), Delay {
181â†’    // null | CLOSED_EMPTY | task | Queue<Runnable>
182â†’    private val _queue = atomic<Any?>(null)
183â†’
184â†’    // Allocated only only once
185â†’    private val _delayed = atomic<DelayedTaskQueue?>(null)
186â†’
187â†’    private val _isCompleted = atomic(false)
188â†’    private var isCompleted
189â†’        get() = _isCompleted.value
190â†’        set(value) { _isCompleted.value = value }
191â†’
192â†’    override val isEmpty: Boolean get() {
193â†’        if (!isUnconfinedQueueEmpty) return false
194â†’        val delayed = _delayed.value
195â†’        if (delayed != null && !delayed.isEmpty) return false
196â†’        return when (val queue = _queue.value) {
197â†’            null -> true
198â†’            is Queue<*> -> queue.isEmpty
199â†’            else -> queue === CLOSED_EMPTY
200â†’        }
201â†’    }
202â†’
203â†’    override val nextTime: Long
204â†’        get() {
205â†’            if (super.nextTime == 0L) return 0L
206â†’            val queue = _queue.value
207â†’            when {
208â†’                queue === null -> {} // empty queue -- proceed
209â†’                queue is Queue<*> -> if (!queue.isEmpty) return 0 // non-empty queue
210â†’                queue === CLOSED_EMPTY -> return Long.MAX_VALUE // no more events -- closed
211â†’                else -> return 0 // non-empty queue
212â†’            }
213â†’            val nextDelayedTask = _delayed.value?.peek() ?: return Long.MAX_VALUE
214â†’            return (nextDelayedTask.nanoTime - nanoTime()).coerceAtLeast(0)
215â†’        }
216â†’
217â†’    override fun shutdown() {
218â†’        // Clean up thread-local reference here -- this event loop is shutting down
219â†’        ThreadLocalEventLoop.resetEventLoop()
220â†’        // We should signal that this event loop should not accept any more tasks
221â†’        // and process queued events (that could have been added after last processNextEvent)
222â†’        isCompleted = true
223â†’        closeQueue()
224â†’        // complete processing of all queued tasks
225â†’        while (processNextEvent() <= 0) { /* spin */ }
226â†’        // reschedule the rest of delayed tasks
227â†’        rescheduleAllDelayed()
228â†’    }
229â†’
230â†’    override fun scheduleResumeAfterDelay(timeMillis: Long, continuation: CancellableContinuation<Unit>) {
231â†’        val timeNanos = delayToNanos(timeMillis)
232â†’        if (timeNanos < MAX_DELAY_NS) {
233â†’            val now = nanoTime()
234â†’            DelayedResumeTask(now + timeNanos, continuation).also { task ->
235â†’                /*
236â†’                 * Order is important here: first we schedule the heap and only then
237â†’                 * publish it to continuation. Otherwise, `DelayedResumeTask` would
238â†’                 * have to know how to be disposed of even when it wasn't scheduled yet.
239â†’                 */
240â†’                schedule(now, task)
241â†’                continuation.disposeOnCancellation(task)
242â†’            }
243â†’        }
244â†’    }
245â†’
246â†’    protected fun scheduleInvokeOnTimeout(timeMillis: Long, block: Runnable): DisposableHandle {
247â†’        val timeNanos = delayToNanos(timeMillis)
248â†’        return if (timeNanos < MAX_DELAY_NS) {
249â†’            val now = nanoTime()
250â†’            DelayedRunnableTask(now + timeNanos, block).also { task ->
251â†’                schedule(now, task)
252â†’            }
253â†’        } else {
254â†’            NonDisposableHandle
255â†’        }
256â†’    }
257â†’
258â†’    override fun processNextEvent(): Long {
259â†’        // unconfined events take priority
260â†’        if (processUnconfinedEvent()) return 0
261â†’        // queue all delayed tasks that are due to be executed
262â†’        val delayed = _delayed.value
263â†’        if (delayed != null && !delayed.isEmpty) {
264â†’            val now = nanoTime()
265â†’            while (true) {
266â†’                // make sure that moving from delayed to queue removes from delayed only after it is added to queue
267â†’                // to make sure that 'isEmpty' and `nextTime` that check both of them
268â†’                // do not transiently report that both delayed and queue are empty during move
269â†’                delayed.removeFirstIf {
270â†’                    if (it.timeToExecute(now)) {
271â†’                        enqueueImpl(it)
272â†’                    } else
273â†’                        false
274â†’                } ?: break // quit loop when nothing more to remove or enqueueImpl returns false on "isComplete"
275â†’            }
276â†’        }
277â†’        // then process one event from queue
278â†’        val task = dequeue()
279â†’        if (task != null) {
280â†’            platformAutoreleasePool { task.run() }
281â†’            return 0
282â†’        }
283â†’        return nextTime
284â†’    }
285â†’
286â†’    final override fun dispatch(context: CoroutineContext, block: Runnable) = enqueue(block)
287â†’
288â†’    open fun enqueue(task: Runnable) {
289â†’        if (enqueueImpl(task)) {
290â†’            // todo: we should unpark only when this delayed task became first in the queue
291â†’            unpark()
292â†’        } else {
293â†’            DefaultExecutor.enqueue(task)
294â†’        }
295â†’    }
296â†’
297â†’    @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
298â†’    private fun enqueueImpl(task: Runnable): Boolean {
299â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
300â†’            if (isCompleted) return false // fail fast if already completed, may still add, but queues will close
301â†’            when (queue) {
302â†’                null -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(null, task)) return true
303â†’                is Queue<*> -> {
304â†’                    when ((queue as Queue<Runnable>).addLast(task)) {
305â†’                        Queue.ADD_SUCCESS -> return true
306â†’                        Queue.ADD_CLOSED -> return false
307â†’                        Queue.ADD_FROZEN -> _queue.compareAndSet(queue, queue.next())
308â†’                    }
309â†’                }
310â†’                else -> when {
311â†’                    queue === CLOSED_EMPTY -> return false
312â†’                    else -> {
313â†’                        // update to full-blown queue to add one more
314â†’                        val newQueue = Queue<Runnable>(Queue.INITIAL_CAPACITY, singleConsumer = true)
315â†’                        newQueue.addLast(queue as Runnable)
316â†’                        newQueue.addLast(task)
317â†’                        if (_queue.compareAndSet(queue, newQueue)) return true
318â†’                    }
319â†’                }
320â†’            }
321â†’        }
322â†’    }
323â†’
324â†’    @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
325â†’    private fun dequeue(): Runnable? {
326â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
327â†’            when (queue) {
328â†’                null -> return null
329â†’                is Queue<*> -> {
330â†’                    val result = (queue as Queue<Runnable>).removeFirstOrNull()
331â†’                    if (result !== Queue.REMOVE_FROZEN) return result as Runnable?
332â†’                    _queue.compareAndSet(queue, queue.next())
333â†’                }
334â†’                else -> when {
335â†’                    queue === CLOSED_EMPTY -> return null
336â†’                    else -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(queue, null)) return queue as Runnable
337â†’                }
338â†’            }
339â†’        }
340â†’    }
341â†’
342â†’    private fun closeQueue() {
343â†’        assert { isCompleted }
344â†’        _queue.loop { queue ->
345â†’            when (queue) {
346â†’                null -> if (_queue.compareAndSet(null, CLOSED_EMPTY)) return
347â†’                is Queue<*> -> {
348â†’                    queue.close()
349â†’                    return
350â†’                }
351â†’                else -> when {
352â†’                    queue === CLOSED_EMPTY -> return
353â†’                    else -> {
354â†’                        // update to full-blown queue to close
355â†’                        val newQueue = Queue<Runnable>(Queue.INITIAL_CAPACITY, singleConsumer = true)
356â†’                        newQueue.addLast(queue as Runnable)
357â†’                        if (_queue.compareAndSet(queue, newQueue)) return
358â†’                    }
359â†’                }
360â†’            }
361â†’        }
362â†’
363â†’    }
364â†’
365â†’    fun schedule(now: Long, delayedTask: DelayedTask) {
366â†’        when (scheduleImpl(now, delayedTask)) {
367â†’            SCHEDULE_OK -> if (shouldUnpark(delayedTask)) unpark()
368â†’            SCHEDULE_COMPLETED -> reschedule(now, delayedTask)
369â†’            SCHEDULE_DISPOSED -> {} // do nothing -- task was already disposed
370â†’            else -> error("unexpected result")
371â†’        }
372â†’    }
373â†’
374â†’    private fun shouldUnpark(task: DelayedTask): Boolean = _delayed.value?.peek() === task
375â†’
376â†’    private fun scheduleImpl(now: Long, delayedTask: DelayedTask): Int {
377â†’        if (isCompleted) return SCHEDULE_COMPLETED
378â†’        val delayedQueue = _delayed.value ?: run {
379â†’            _delayed.compareAndSet(null, DelayedTaskQueue(now))
380â†’            _delayed.value!!
381â†’        }
382â†’        return delayedTask.scheduleTask(now, delayedQueue, this)
383â†’    }
384â†’
385â†’    // It performs "hard" shutdown for test cleanup purposes
386â†’    protected fun resetAll() {
387â†’        _queue.value = null
388â†’        _delayed.value = null
389â†’    }
390â†’
391â†’    // This is a "soft" (normal) shutdown
392â†’    private fun rescheduleAllDelayed() {
393â†’        val now = nanoTime()
394â†’        while (true) {
395â†’            /*
396â†’             * `removeFirstOrNull` below is the only operation on DelayedTask & ThreadSafeHeap that is not
397â†’             * synchronized on DelayedTask itself. All other operation are synchronized both on
398â†’             * DelayedTask & ThreadSafeHeap instances (in this order). It is still safe, because `dispose`
399â†’             * first removes DelayedTask from the heap (under synchronization) then
400â†’             * assign "_heap = DISPOSED_TASK", so there cannot be ever a race to _heap reference update.
401â†’             */
402â†’            val delayedTask = _delayed.value?.removeFirstOrNull() ?: break
403â†’            reschedule(now, delayedTask)
404â†’        }
405â†’    }
406â†’
407â†’    internal abstract class DelayedTask(
408â†’        /**
409â†’         * This field can be only modified in [scheduleTask] before putting this DelayedTask
410â†’         * into heap to avoid overflow and corruption of heap data structure.
411â†’         */
412â†’        @JvmField var nanoTime: Long
413â†’    ) : Runnable, Comparable<DelayedTask>, DisposableHandle, ThreadSafeHeapNode, SynchronizedObject() {
414â†’        @Volatile
415â†’        private var _heap: Any? = null // null | ThreadSafeHeap | DISPOSED_TASK
416â†’
417â†’        override var heap: ThreadSafeHeap<*>?
418â†’            get() = _heap as? ThreadSafeHeap<*>
419â†’            set(value) {
420â†’                require(_heap !== DISPOSED_TASK) // this can never happen, it is always checked before adding/removing
421â†’                _heap = value
422â†’            }
423â†’
424â†’        override var index: Int = -1
425â†’
426â†’        override fun compareTo(other: DelayedTask): Int {
427â†’            val dTime = nanoTime - other.nanoTime
428â†’            return when {
429â†’                dTime > 0 -> 1
430â†’                dTime < 0 -> -1
431â†’                else -> 0
432â†’            }
433â†’        }
434â†’
435â†’        fun timeToExecute(now: Long): Boolean = now - nanoTime >= 0L
436â†’
437â†’        fun scheduleTask(now: Long, delayed: DelayedTaskQueue, eventLoop: EventLoopImplBase): Int = synchronized<Int>(this) {
438â†’            if (_heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return SCHEDULE_DISPOSED // don't add -- was already disposed
439â†’            delayed.addLastIf(this) { firstTask ->
440â†’                if (eventLoop.isCompleted) return SCHEDULE_COMPLETED // non-local return from scheduleTask
441â†’                /**
442â†’                 * We are about to add new task and we have to make sure that [DelayedTaskQueue]
443â†’                 * invariant is maintained. The code in this lambda is additionally executed under
444â†’                 * the lock of [DelayedTaskQueue] and working with [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] here is thread-safe.
445â†’                 */
446â†’                if (firstTask == null) {
447â†’                    /**
448â†’                     * When adding the first delayed task we simply update queue's [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] to
449â†’                     * the current now time even if that means "going backwards in time". This makes the structure
450â†’                     * self-correcting in spite of wild jumps in `nanoTime()` measurements once all delayed tasks
451â†’                     * are removed from the delayed queue for execution.
452â†’                     */
453â†’                    delayed.timeNow = now
454â†’                } else {
455â†’                    /**
456â†’                     * Carefully update [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] so that it does not sweep past first's tasks time
457â†’                     * and only goes forward in time. We cannot let it go backwards in time or invariant can be
458â†’                     * violated for tasks that were already scheduled.
459â†’                     */
460â†’                    val firstTime = firstTask.nanoTime
461â†’                    // compute min(now, firstTime) using a wrap-safe check
462â†’                    val minTime = if (firstTime - now >= 0) now else firstTime
463â†’                    // update timeNow only when going forward in time
464â†’                    if (minTime - delayed.timeNow > 0) delayed.timeNow = minTime
465â†’                }
466â†’                /**
467â†’                 * Here [DelayedTaskQueue.timeNow] was already modified and we have to double-check that newly added
468â†’                 * task does not violate [DelayedTaskQueue] invariant because of that. Note also that this scheduleTask
469â†’                 * function can be called to reschedule from one queue to another and this might be another reason
470â†’                 * where new task's time might now violate invariant.
471â†’                 * We correct invariant violation (if any) by simply changing this task's time to now.
472â†’                 */
473â†’                if (nanoTime - delayed.timeNow < 0) nanoTime = delayed.timeNow
474â†’                true
475â†’            }
476â†’            return SCHEDULE_OK
477â†’        }
478â†’
479â†’        final override fun dispose(): Unit = synchronized(this) {
480â†’            val heap = _heap
481â†’            if (heap === DISPOSED_TASK) return // already disposed
482â†’            (heap as? DelayedTaskQueue)?.remove(this) // remove if it is in heap (first)
483â†’            _heap = DISPOSED_TASK // never add again to any heap
484â†’        }
485â†’
486â†’        override fun toString(): String = "Delayed[nanos=$nanoTime]"
487â†’    }
488â†’
489â†’    private inner class DelayedResumeTask(
490â†’        nanoTime: Long,
491â†’        private val cont: CancellableContinuation<Unit>
492â†’    ) : DelayedTask(nanoTime) {
493â†’        override fun run() { with(cont) { resumeUndispatched(Unit) } }
494â†’        override fun toString(): String = super.toString() + cont.toString()
495â†’    }
496â†’
497â†’    private class DelayedRunnableTask(
498â†’        nanoTime: Long,
499â†’        private val block: Runnable
500â†’    ) : DelayedTask(nanoTime) {
501â†’        override fun run() { block.run() }
502â†’        override fun toString(): String = super.toString() + block.toString()
503â†’    }
504â†’
505â†’    /**
506â†’     * Delayed task queue maintains stable time-comparision invariant despite potential wraparounds in
507â†’     * long nano time measurements by maintaining last observed [timeNow]. It protects the integrity of the
508â†’     * heap data structure in spite of potential non-monotonicity of `nanoTime()` source.
509â†’     * The invariant is that for every scheduled [DelayedTask]:
510â†’     *
511â†’     * ```
512â†’     * delayedTask.nanoTime - timeNow >= 0
513â†’     * ```
514â†’     *
515â†’     * So the comparison of scheduled tasks via [DelayedTask.compareTo] is always stable as
516â†’     * scheduled [DelayedTask.nanoTime] can be at most [Long.MAX_VALUE] apart. This invariant is maintained when
517â†’     * new tasks are added by [DelayedTask.scheduleTask] function and it cannot be violated when tasks are removed
518â†’     * (so there is nothing special to do there).
519â†’     */
520â†’    internal class DelayedTaskQueue(
521â†’        @JvmField var timeNow: Long
522â†’    ) : ThreadSafeHeap<DelayedTask>()
523â†’}
524â†’
525â†’internal expect fun createEventLoop(): EventLoop
526â†’
527â†’internal expect fun nanoTime(): Long
528â†’
529â†’internal expect object DefaultExecutor {
530â†’    fun enqueue(task: Runnable)
531â†’}
532â†’
533â†’/**
534â†’ * Used by Darwin targets to wrap a [Runnable.run] call in an Objective-C Autorelease Pool. It is a no-op on JVM, JS and
535â†’ * non-Darwin native targets.
536â†’ *
537â†’ * Coroutines on Darwin targets can call into the Objective-C world, where a callee may push a to-be-returned object to
538â†’ * the Autorelease Pool, so as to avoid a premature ARC release before it reaches the caller. This means the pool must
539â†’ * be eventually drained to avoid leaks. Since Kotlin Coroutines does not use [NSRunLoop], which provides automatic
540â†’ * pool management, it must manage the pool creation and pool drainage manually.
541â†’ */
542â†’internal expect inline fun platformAutoreleasePool(crossinline block: () -> Unit)
543â†’

content.text:
I have resolved the issue where `delay(Duration)` and other duration-based APIs (like `withTimeout`, `onTimeout`, and `WhileSubscribed`) were losing precision by incorrectly rounding down sub-millisecond durations.

The core of the fix is an updated `toDelayMillis()` internal extension in `Delay.kt`, which now correctly rounds up to the next millisecond if any nanosecond component is present. This ensures that a delay will never finish earlier than requested, even by a fraction of a millisecond.

### Changes:
- **`kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/Delay.kt`**: Updated `toDelayMillis()` to round up sub-millisecond durations. Added `import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.milliseconds` to support the implementation.
- **`kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/flow/SharingStarted.kt`**: Updated `WhileSubscribed` to use `toDelayMillis()` instead of `inWholeMilliseconds` for both `stopTimeout` and `replayExpiration`.
- **`kotlinx-coroutines-test/common/src/TestCoroutineScheduler.kt`**: Updated `advanceTimeBy(Duration)` to round up sub-millisecond durations, ensuring consistency between virtual time advancement and `delay` behavior.
- **`kotlinx-coroutines-core/jdk8/src/time/Time.kt`**: Updated the `java.time.Duration` adapter's `coerceToMillis()` function to also round up sub-millisecond durations, maintaining consistency for JVM users.
- **`kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/test/DelayPrecisionTest.kt`**: Created a new test file to verify the fix for both the internal conversion logic and the actual `delay` behavior.

These changes ensure that all `Duration`-based delay and timeout functions in `kotlinx.coroutines` behave consistently and honor the full precision of the provided `Duration` by rounding up to the nearest millisecond when necessary.

  4â†’
  5â†’package kotlinx.coroutines
  6â†’
  7â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.selects.*
  8â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
  9â†’import kotlin.time.*
 10â†’
 11â†’/**
 12â†’ * This dispatcher _feature_ is implemented by [CoroutineDispatcher] implementations that natively support
 13â†’ * scheduled execution of tasks.
 14â†’ *
 15â†’ * Implementation of this interface affects operation of
 16â†’ * [delay][kotlinx.coroutines.delay] and [withTimeout] functions.
 17â†’ *
 18â†’ * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
 19â†’ */
 20â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
 21â†’public interface Delay {
 22â†’
 23â†’    /** @suppress **/
 24â†’    @Deprecated(
 25â†’        message = "Deprecated without replacement as an internal method never intended for public use",
 26â†’        level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR
 27â†’    ) // Error since 1.6.0
 28â†’    public suspend fun delay(time: Long) {
 29â†’        if (time <= 0) return // don't delay
 30â†’        return suspendCancellableCoroutine { scheduleResumeAfterDelay(time, it) }
 31â†’    }
 32â†’
 33â†’    /**
 34â†’     * Schedules resume of a specified [continuation] after a specified delay [timeMillis].
 35â†’     *
 36â†’     * Continuation **must be scheduled** to resume even if it is already cancelled, because a cancellation is just
 37â†’     * an exception that the coroutine that used `delay` might wanted to catch and process. It might
 38â†’     * need to close some resources in its `finally` blocks, for example.
 39â†’     *
 40â†’     * This implementation is supposed to use dispatcher's native ability for scheduled execution in its thread(s).
 41â†’     * In order to avoid an extra delay of execution, the following code shall be used to resume this
 42â†’     * [continuation] when the code is already executing in the appropriate thread:
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * ```kotlin
 45â†’     * with(continuation) { resumeUndispatchedWith(Unit) }
 46â†’     * ```
 47â†’     */
 48â†’    public fun scheduleResumeAfterDelay(timeMillis: Long, continuation: CancellableContinuation<Unit>)
 49â†’
 50â†’    /**
 51â†’     * Schedules invocation of a specified [block] after a specified delay [timeMillis].
 52â†’     * The resulting [DisposableHandle] can be used to [dispose][DisposableHandle.dispose] of this invocation
 53â†’     * request if it is not needed anymore.
 54â†’     */
 55â†’    public fun invokeOnTimeout(timeMillis: Long, block: Runnable, context: CoroutineContext): DisposableHandle =
 56â†’        DefaultDelay.invokeOnTimeout(timeMillis, block, context)
 57â†’}
 58â†’
 59â†’/**
 60â†’ * Enhanced [Delay] interface that provides additional diagnostics for [withTimeout].
 61â†’ * Is going to be removed once there is proper JVM-default support.
 62â†’ * Then we'll be able put this function into [Delay] without breaking binary compatibility.
 63â†’ */
 64â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
 65â†’internal interface DelayWithTimeoutDiagnostics : Delay {
 66â†’    /**
 67â†’     * Returns a string that explains that the timeout has occurred, and explains what can be done about it.
 68â†’     */
 69â†’    fun timeoutMessage(timeout: Duration): String
 70â†’}
 71â†’
 72â†’/**
 73â†’ * Suspends until cancellation, in which case it will throw a [CancellationException].
 74â†’ *
 75â†’ * This function returns [Nothing], so it can be used in any coroutine,
 76â†’ * regardless of the required return type.
 77â†’ *
 78â†’ * Usage example in callback adapting code:
 79â†’ *
 80â†’ * ```kotlin
 81â†’ * fun currentTemperature(): Flow<Temperature> = callbackFlow {
 82â†’ *     val callback = SensorCallback { degreesCelsius: Double ->
 83â†’ *         trySend(Temperature.celsius(degreesCelsius))
 84â†’ *     }
 85â†’ *     try {
 86â†’ *         registerSensorCallback(callback)
 87â†’ *         awaitCancellation() // Suspends to keep getting updates until cancellation.
 88â†’ *     } finally {
 89â†’ *         unregisterSensorCallback(callback)
 90â†’ *     }
 91â†’ * }
 92â†’ * ```
 93â†’ *
 94â†’ * Usage example in (non declarative) UI code:
 95â†’ *
 96â†’ * ```kotlin
 97â†’ * suspend fun showStuffUntilCancelled(content: Stuff): Nothing {
 98â†’ *     someSubView.text = content.title
 99â†’ *     anotherSubView.text = content.description
100â†’ *     someView.visibleInScope {
101â†’ *         awaitCancellation() // Suspends so the view stays visible.
102â†’ *     }
103â†’ * }
104â†’ * ```
105â†’ */
106â†’public suspend fun awaitCancellation(): Nothing = suspendCancellableCoroutine {}
107â†’
108â†’/**
109â†’ * Delays coroutine for a given time without blocking a thread and resumes it after a specified time.
110â†’ * If the given [timeMillis] is non-positive, this function returns immediately.
111â†’ *
112â†’ * This suspending function is cancellable.
113â†’ * If the [Job] of the current coroutine is cancelled or completed while this suspending function is waiting, this function
114â†’ * immediately resumes with [CancellationException].
115â†’ * There is a **prompt cancellation guarantee**. If the job was cancelled while this function was
116â†’ * suspended, it will not resume successfully. See [suspendCancellableCoroutine] documentation for low-level details.
117â†’ *
118â†’ * If you want to delay forever (until cancellation), consider using [awaitCancellation] instead.
119â†’ *
120â†’ * Note that delay can be used in [select] invocation with [onTimeout][SelectBuilder.onTimeout] clause.
121â†’ *
122â†’ * Implementation note: how exactly time is tracked is an implementation detail of [CoroutineDispatcher] in the context.
123â†’ * @param timeMillis time in milliseconds.
124â†’ */
125â†’public suspend fun delay(timeMillis: Long) {
126â†’    if (timeMillis <= 0) return // don't delay
127â†’    return suspendCancellableCoroutine sc@ { cont: CancellableContinuation<Unit> ->
128â†’        // if timeMillis == Long.MAX_VALUE then just wait forever like awaitCancellation, don't schedule.
129â†’        if (timeMillis < Long.MAX_VALUE) {
130â†’            cont.context.delay.scheduleResumeAfterDelay(timeMillis, cont)
131â†’        }
132â†’    }
133â†’}
134â†’
135â†’/**
136â†’ * Delays coroutine for a given [duration] without blocking a thread and resumes it after the specified time.
137â†’ * If the given [duration] is non-positive, this function returns immediately.
138â†’ *
139â†’ * This suspending function is cancellable.
140â†’ * If the [Job] of the current coroutine is cancelled or completed while this suspending function is waiting, this function
141â†’ * immediately resumes with [CancellationException].
142â†’ * There is a **prompt cancellation guarantee**. If the job was cancelled while this function was
143â†’ * suspended, it will not resume successfully. See [suspendCancellableCoroutine] documentation for low-level details.
144â†’ *
145â†’ * If you want to delay forever (until cancellation), consider using [awaitCancellation] instead.
146â†’ *
147â†’ * Note that delay can be used in [select] invocation with [onTimeout][SelectBuilder.onTimeout] clause.
148â†’ *
149â†’ * Implementation note: how exactly time is tracked is an implementation detail of [CoroutineDispatcher] in the context.
150â†’ */
151â†’public suspend fun delay(duration: Duration): Unit = delay(duration.toDelayMillis())
152â†’
153â†’/** Returns [Delay] implementation of the given context */
154â†’internal val CoroutineContext.delay: Delay get() = get(ContinuationInterceptor) as? Delay ?: DefaultDelay
155â†’
156â†’/**
157â†’ * Convert this duration to its millisecond value.
158â†’ * Positive durations are coerced at least `1`.
159â†’ */
160â†’internal fun Duration.toDelayMillis(): Long =
161â†’    if (this > Duration.ZERO) inWholeMilliseconds.coerceAtLeast(1) else 0

33â†’            expect(4)
34â†’        }
35â†’
36â†’        expect(1)
37â†’        yield()

48â†’            expect(4)
49â†’        }
50â†’

63â†’            expectUnreached()

68â†’        require(deferred.isActive)

72â†’    }
73â†’}

 32â†’/**
 33â†’ * Returns a flow that mirrors the original flow, but filters out values
 34â†’ * that are followed by the newer values within the given [timeout][timeoutMillis].
 35â†’ * The latest value is always emitted.
 36â†’ *
 37â†’ * Example:
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * ```kotlin
 40â†’ * flow {
 41â†’ *     emit(1)
 42â†’ *     delay(90)
 43â†’ *     emit(2)
 44â†’ *     delay(90)
 45â†’ *     emit(3)
 46â†’ *     delay(1010)
 47â†’ *     emit(4)
 48â†’ *     delay(1010)
 49â†’ *     emit(5)
 50â†’ * }.debounce(1000)
 51â†’ * ```
 52â†’ * <!--- KNIT example-delay-01.kt -->
 53â†’ *
 54â†’ * produces the following emissions
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * ```text
 57â†’ * 3, 4, 5
 58â†’ * ```
 59â†’ * <!--- TEST -->
 60â†’ *
 61â†’ * Note that the resulting flow does not emit anything as long as the original flow emits
 62â†’ * items faster than every [timeoutMillis] milliseconds.
 63â†’ */
 64â†’@FlowPreview
 65â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.debounce(timeoutMillis: Long): Flow<T> {
 66â†’    require(timeoutMillis >= 0L) { "Debounce timeout should not be negative" }
 67â†’    if (timeoutMillis == 0L) return this
 68â†’    return debounceInternal { timeoutMillis }
 69â†’}

161â†’ *

163â†’ *

165â†’ * flow {
166â†’ *     emit(1)
167â†’ *     delay(90.milliseconds)
168â†’ *     emit(2)

172â†’ *     emit(4)
173â†’ *     delay(1010.milliseconds)
174â†’ *     emit(5)
175â†’ * }.debounce {
176â†’ *     if (it == 1) {
177â†’ *         0.milliseconds
178â†’ *     } else {
179â†’ *         1000.milliseconds
180â†’ *     }
181â†’ * }
182â†’ * ```
183â†’ * <!--- KNIT example-delay-duration-02.kt -->
184â†’ *
185â†’ * produces the following emissions
186â†’ *
187â†’ * ```text
